topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The heavy oak door clicked shut behind me, the sound echoing in the otherwise silent study. I stood frozen, my hand still hovering near the knob, the air thick and suddenly too thin. “Professor Alistair?” I managed, my voice sounding ridiculously small in the vast room. I turned slowly, expecting a familiar, reassuring smile, a nod of acknowledgment. Instead, there was only an empty armchair, the leather creased exactly as it always did, and the faint scent of old paper lingering where he used to sit. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, a sudden, sharp awareness of my own awkwardness. I shuffled my feet, the sound loud on the polished floorboards. I wanted to backtrack, to stammer out an apology for interrupting, but the words caught in my throat. He was gone. Simply gone.

[story 2]

I reread the typed notification three times, the screen glare making the text swim. 'Effective immediately, Professor Alistair has retired.' It was a clinical statement, devoid of warmth, and it hit me like a physical blow. I felt my cheeks flush instantly, a hot tide rising from my neck up to my hairline. I clutched the paper so tightly my knuckles turned white. I glanced around the office, at the framed photographs lining the wall—pictures of him laughing, looking intensely engaged in a lecture. It felt like a spotlight had suddenly been switched on, illuminating every clumsy, unpolished aspect of my recent interactions with him. I cleared my throat, a dry, rasping sound, and tried to muster a casual tone, but it came out tight and strained. "Right," I mumbled, turning abruptly toward the door, needing to escape the sudden, suffocating silence.

[story 3]

When I finally found him in the hallway, leaning against the bookcase, he looked up, his expression perfectly composed, entirely unreadable. “Leaving so soon, Elara?” he asked, his tone smooth, almost too easy. My carefully rehearsed greeting dissolved. I felt a sudden, intense need to smooth down my sweater, a nervous habit I couldn't shake. My gaze dropped to the floor, tracing the pattern of the rug with my eyes. I wanted to ask him, 'Wait, didn't you say anything?' but the question felt monumental, too clumsy for the situation. I managed a weak, tight smile, the muscles in my face feeling unfamiliar and stiff. "Just... processing," I replied, the word sounding hollow even to my own ears. I shifted my weight from one foot to the other, acutely aware of the silence stretching between us, heavy with unspoken farewells.
